1:
"Now concerning the things whereof ye wrote unto me: It is good for a man not to touch a woman." The witnesses note Paul's opening on marriage and celibacy. On "the things whereof ye wrote," the grammatical voices note the occasion. Barnes and Robertson note Paul now turns to answer questions the Corinthians had written him — the first concerning marriage and the single life.

On "it is good for a man not to touch a woman," the witnesses note the statement. Barnes, Calvin, and Robertson note "to touch a woman" is a delicate idiom for marriage (or sexual relations); Paul affirms that, in certain circumstances, celibacy is "good" (expedient, honourable, advantageous) — though, as the next verses show, not binding nor superior in itself. Some take the words as a Corinthian ascetic maxim Paul is qualifying.

The eastern Chrysostom and Reformed Calvin note the balance: Paul does not condemn marriage nor exalt celibacy as holier, but commends the single state as good for those gifted for it, immediately balancing it with marriage's place (v.2). The verse displays Paul's opening on marriage and celibacy.

The witnesses agree: Paul turns to answer the Corinthians' written questions, the first on marriage (Barnes, Robertson); and affirms that in certain circumstances celibacy is "good" (expedient, honourable) — "to touch a woman" a delicate idiom for marriage — though not binding nor superior in itself, some taking the words as a Corinthian maxim Paul qualifies (Barnes, Calvin, Robertson). Chrysostom and Calvin note Paul neither condemns marriage nor exalts celibacy as holier. The verse displays Paul's opening. The verse marks the occasion: their written questions. The verse displays the statement: good not to touch a woman. The verse displays the balance: not binding. The verse displays the qualification: marriage's place follows. The opening displays celibacy commended as good, not superior. The verse displays the question: on marriage. The verse displays the good: celibacy for some. The verse displays the balance: with marriage. Paul's opening on marriage and celibacy displays his measured judgment: answering the Corinthians' question, he grants that celibacy is good and expedient in certain circumstances, without making it binding or holier than marriage. The verse displays the apostle's balanced approach to the single life, immediately qualified by the proper place of marriage (v.2).
2:
"Nevertheless, to avoid fornication, let every man have his own wife, and let every woman have her own husband." The witnesses note marriage as God's provision against fornication. On "to avoid fornication," the grammatical voices note the reason. Barnes and Robertson note Paul, having granted that celibacy is good, immediately balances it: because of the danger of fornication (the prevalence of sexual sin, especially at Corinth), marriage is the proper and God-ordained provision.

On "let every man have his own wife," the witnesses note the rule. Barnes and Calvin note each man is to have his own wife, and each woman her own husband — affirming monogamous marriage as the divinely appointed remedy against incontinence.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the balance: Paul does not present marriage merely as a remedy for lust, but here, against the Corinthian danger, stresses its use in preserving purity; celibacy is not for all. The verse displays marriage as God's provision against fornication.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul, having granted celibacy is good, balances it — because of the danger of fornication (especially at Corinth), marriage is the proper God-ordained provision (Barnes, Robertson); each man to have his own wife and each woman her own husband, affirming monogamous marriage as the appointed remedy against incontinence (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin and Wesley note Paul does not reduce marriage to a remedy for lust, but here stresses its use in preserving purity; celibacy is not for all. The verse displays marriage as God's provision. The verse marks the reason: to avoid fornication. The verse displays the rule: his own wife. The verse displays the remedy: against incontinence. The verse displays the balance: celibacy not for all. Marriage displays the divinely appointed safeguard of purity. The verse displays the danger: fornication. The verse displays the provision: monogamous marriage. The verse displays the appointment: of God. Marriage as God's provision against fornication displays its divinely appointed use in preserving purity: against the prevalence of sexual sin, each is to have his own spouse, marriage being the proper remedy against incontinence — so that celibacy, though good, is not for all. The verse displays the place of marriage alongside the single life, and defines the mutual duty within it (vv.3-5).
3:
"Let the husband render unto the wife due benevolence: and likewise also the wife unto the husband." The brief verse notes the mutual conjugal duty of husband and wife. The grammatical voices note the obligation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul enjoins the "due benevolence" (the conjugal duty, the rights of the marriage bed) which the husband owes the wife and the wife the husband — a mutual debt within marriage.

On "and likewise the wife," the witnesses note the equality. Barnes and Calvin note the striking mutuality: the duty is reciprocal, the wife owing it to the husband as the husband to the wife — a notable equality of obligation in an age that stressed the wife's subjection.

The Reformed Calvin notes the point: marriage carries mutual obligations that neither party may withhold; the conjugal due is owed by both alike. The verse displays the mutual conjugal duty of husband and wife.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul enjoins the "due benevolence" (the conjugal duty, the rights of the marriage bed) which the husband owes the wife and the wife the husband — a mutual debt (Barnes, Robertson); the duty reciprocal, a notable equality of obligation in an age stressing the wife's subjection (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes marriage carries mutual obligations neither may withhold. The verse displays the mutual conjugal duty. The verse marks the obligation: due benevolence. The verse displays the mutuality: likewise the wife. The verse displays the equality: a reciprocal debt. The verse displays the principle: owed by both. The duty displays the reciprocal obligation of the marriage bond. The verse displays the husband: owing the wife. The verse displays the wife: owing the husband. The verse displays the equality: of obligation. The mutual conjugal duty of husband and wife displays the reciprocal obligation of marriage: the due benevolence of the marriage bed is owed by each to the other alike, a striking equality of duty within the bond. The verse displays the mutual rights of the married, and grounds them in their mutual authority over each other (v.4). That the obligation runs in both directions, the wife owing the husband no less than the husband the wife, is the more remarkable in an age that thought chiefly of the wife as subject, and it sets the marriage bond upon a footing of genuine mutual self-giving rather than of one-sided demand.
4:
"The wife hath not power of her own body, but the husband: and likewise also the husband hath not power of his own body, but the wife." The brief verse notes the mutual authority of spouses over each other's bodies. The grammatical voices note the principle. Barnes and Robertson note Paul states the ground of the conjugal duty: the wife has not sole power (authority, control) over her own body, but the husband shares it — and, equally, the husband has not sole power over his own body, but the wife.

On the mutuality, the witnesses note the equality. Barnes notes the reciprocity is exact: each has yielded to the other a claim over their own body; neither may act as if wholly independent.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the bond: in marriage the two become one flesh, and this mutual surrender of the body is the basis of the conjugal duty. The verse displays the mutual authority of spouses over each other's bodies.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul states the ground of the conjugal duty — the wife has not sole power over her own body but the husband shares it, and equally the husband has not sole power over his own body but the wife (Barnes, Robertson); the reciprocity exact, each having yielded the other a claim, neither wholly independent (Barnes). Wesley notes that in marriage the two become one flesh, this mutual surrender the basis of the duty. The verse displays the mutual authority. The verse marks the principle: not sole power over one's body. The verse displays the mutuality: likewise the husband. The verse displays the equality: a shared claim. The verse displays the bond: one flesh. The authority displays the mutual surrender of the marriage bond. The verse displays the wife: yielding to the husband. The verse displays the husband: yielding to the wife. The verse displays the union: one flesh. The mutual authority of spouses over each other's bodies displays the reciprocal surrender at the heart of marriage: neither has sole power over their own body, but each has yielded a claim to the other, the two being one flesh. The verse displays the basis of the conjugal duty, and warns against withholding it except by consent (v.5).
5:
"Defraud ye not one the other, except it be with consent for a time, that ye may give yourselves to fasting and prayer; and come together again, that Satan tempt you not for your incontinency." The witnesses note the rule against withholding the conjugal due, except by consent for prayer. On "defraud ye not one the other," the grammatical voices note the prohibition. Barnes and Robertson note Paul forbids defrauding (withholding the conjugal due) one another — neither spouse may deprive the other of their right.

On "except with consent for a time... for fasting and prayer," the witnesses note the exception. Barnes and Calvin note the one allowed exception: by mutual consent, for a limited time, that they may devote themselves to fasting and prayer — a temporary abstinence for a spiritual purpose, never one-sided or permanent.

On "come together again, that Satan tempt you not," the Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the caution: they are to resume the marriage relation, lest prolonged abstinence give Satan occasion to tempt them through incontinency. The verse displays the rule against withholding the conjugal due except by consent for prayer.

The witnesses agree: Paul forbids defrauding (withholding the conjugal due) one another, neither spouse depriving the other of their right (Barnes, Robertson); the one exception being by mutual consent, for a limited time, that they may give themselves to fasting and prayer — a temporary abstinence for a spiritual purpose, never one-sided or permanent (Barnes, Calvin); after which they are to come together again, lest prolonged abstinence give Satan occasion to tempt them through incontinency (Calvin, Wesley). The verse displays the rule against withholding. The verse marks the prohibition: defraud not. The verse displays the exception: consent for prayer. The verse displays the limit: for a time. The verse displays the caution: lest Satan tempt. The rule displays the safeguard of mutual conjugal duty. The verse displays the defrauding: forbidden. The verse displays the consent: required. The verse displays the resumption: lest temptation come. The rule against withholding the conjugal due, except by consent for prayer, displays the wise safeguard of the marriage bond: neither may defraud the other, save by mutual agreement for a season of prayer, after which they are to come together again, lest Satan exploit prolonged abstinence to tempt them. The verse displays the balance of spiritual devotion and marital duty, which Paul then frames as concession not command (v.6).
6:
"But I speak this by permission, and not of commandment." The brief verse notes Paul's word as concession, not command. The grammatical voices note the qualification. Barnes and Robertson note Paul adds that what he has just said he speaks "by permission" (as a concession, indulgence) and "not of commandment" — that is, he allows, rather than commands, certain things, leaving liberty.

On the reference, the witnesses debate what the concession covers. Barnes notes it likely refers to the foregoing counsel about marriage and the conjugal relation (vv.2-5) — Paul permits marriage (and these arrangements) as a concession to human weakness, without commanding that all must marry; some refer it to the temporary abstinence of v.5.

The Reformed Calvin notes the care: Paul distinguishes his apostolic concessions from binding commands, that the Corinthians might not take his advice as a universal law. The verse displays Paul's word as concession, not command.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul adds that what he has said he speaks "by permission" (as a concession, indulgence) and "not of commandment" — allowing rather than commanding, leaving liberty (Barnes, Robertson); the concession likely covering the foregoing counsel about marriage and the conjugal relation (Paul permits marriage as a concession to human weakness, not commanding all must marry), some referring it to the abstinence of v.5 (Barnes). Calvin notes Paul distinguishes concessions from binding commands. The verse displays the word as concession. The verse marks the qualification: by permission. The verse displays the negation: not of commandment. The verse displays the reference: the foregoing counsel. The verse displays the care: not a universal law. The concession displays Paul's distinction of counsel from command. The verse displays the permission: granted. The verse displays the liberty: left. The verse displays the care: not binding all. Paul's word as concession, not command, displays his careful distinction between apostolic counsel and binding precept: what he has said about marriage and the conjugal relation he speaks as a permission, an indulgence to human weakness, not as a law requiring all to marry. The verse displays the liberty Paul preserves, and states his own preference for the single state (v.7).
7:
"For I would that all men were even as I myself. But every man hath his proper gift of God, one after this manner, and another after that." The witnesses note Paul's wish and the diversity of gifts. On "I would that all men were even as I myself," the grammatical voices note the preference. Barnes and Robertson note Paul expresses his personal wish that all were as he is — that is, unmarried (continent, free for undivided service) — esteeming the single state, for those able, advantageous.

On "every man hath his proper gift of God," the witnesses note the qualification. Barnes and Calvin note Paul at once qualifies: each has his own gift (charisma) from God — to one the gift of continence (celibacy), to another the gift suited to marriage; the capacity for either is God's bestowal, not a matter of merit.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the point: celibacy is a special gift, not given to all nor to be presumed; those without it should marry, and neither state is holier — both are gifts of God. The verse displays Paul's wish and the diversity of gifts.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul wishes all were as he is — unmarried, continent, free for undivided service — esteeming the single state advantageous for those able (Barnes, Robertson); but at once qualifies that each has his own gift (charisma) from God, to one continence and to another the capacity for marriage, neither a matter of merit (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin and Wesley note celibacy is a special gift, not given to all nor holier; those without it should marry. The verse displays Paul's wish and the gifts. The verse marks the wish: all as himself. The verse displays the qualification: each his own gift. The verse displays the diversity: continence or marriage. The verse displays the principle: both gifts of God. The wish displays celibacy as a gift, not a universal rule. The verse displays the preference: the single state. The verse displays the gift: from God. The verse displays the diversity: one or the other. Paul's wish and the diversity of gifts display celibacy as a special bestowal, not a universal standard: though he would that all shared his continent freedom, he grants that each has his own gift from God — to one the capacity for the single life, to another for marriage — neither holier than the other. The verse displays the gift of continence as God's, and applies the counsel to the unmarried and widows (vv.8-9).
8:
"I say therefore to the unmarried and widows, It is good for them if they abide even as I." The brief verse notes the counsel to the unmarried and widows to abide single. The grammatical voices note the address. Barnes and Robertson note Paul now directs counsel specifically to the unmarried (the never-married, or the single generally) and to widows: it is good for them to abide (remain) even as he is — that is, single.

On "good... if they abide even as I," the witnesses note the commendation. Barnes notes Paul again commends the single state as good (advantageous) for these, for the reasons he will develop — freedom from care and undivided devotion.

The eastern Cyril and Reformed Calvin note the qualification (anticipating v.9): the counsel is for those who can remain continent; it is good, not commanded, and yields to the need to marry where continence is lacking. The verse displays the counsel to the unmarried and widows to abide single.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul directs counsel to the unmarried (the never-married or single generally) and widows — it is good for them to abide single, even as he is (Barnes, Robertson); commending the single state as advantageous for these, for freedom from care and undivided devotion (Barnes). Cyril and Calvin note the counsel is for those who can remain continent — good, not commanded, yielding to the need to marry where continence is lacking. The verse displays the counsel to the single. The verse marks the address: the unmarried and widows. The verse displays the commendation: good to abide. The verse displays the model: as Paul. The verse displays the qualification: for those able. The counsel displays the single state commended to the unmarried. The verse displays the unmarried: addressed. The verse displays the widows: addressed. The verse displays the good: abiding single. The counsel to the unmarried and widows to abide single displays Paul's commendation of the single state to those able to keep it: it is good for them to remain as he is, free for undivided devotion — though, as the next verse shows, this yields where continence is lacking. The verse displays the advantage of the single life for the continent, and provides for those who cannot contain (v.9).
9:
"But if they cannot contain, let them marry: for it is better to marry than to burn." The witnesses note the provision: better to marry than to burn. On "if they cannot contain, let them marry," the grammatical voices note the exception. Barnes and Robertson note Paul provides for those lacking the gift of continence: if they cannot contain (exercise self-control, remain chaste in the single state), let them marry — marriage being the proper, lawful course.

On "better to marry than to burn," the witnesses note the comparison. Barnes and Calvin note "to burn" means to be inflamed with desire (the fire of unsatisfied passion), perhaps tending to sin; it is far better to marry than to be thus consumed and endangered.

The Reformed Calvin and eastern Cyril note the balance: marriage is not a lesser holiness but the divinely provided and honourable remedy; those without the gift of continence sin not at all but do well to marry. The verse displays the provision: better to marry than to burn.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul provides for those lacking the gift of continence — if they cannot contain (remain chaste single), let them marry, marriage being the proper lawful course (Barnes, Robertson); for "to burn" (to be inflamed with desire, the fire of unsatisfied passion, tending to sin) is far worse, and it is better to marry than to be thus consumed (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin and Cyril note marriage is not a lesser holiness but the honourable, divinely provided remedy. The verse displays the provision. The verse marks the exception: if they cannot contain. The verse displays the course: let them marry. The verse displays the comparison: better than to burn. The verse displays the balance: marriage honourable. The provision displays marriage as the remedy for those lacking continence. The verse displays the inability: cannot contain. The verse displays the remedy: marriage. The verse displays the comparison: better to marry. The provision that it is better to marry than to burn displays marriage as the honourable remedy for those without the gift of continence: rather than be consumed by unsatisfied desire and endangered, they should marry, which is no sin but the divinely appointed course. The verse displays the proper place of marriage for the incontinent, and turns to the Lord's command for the married (vv.10-11).
10:
"And unto the married I command, yet not I, but the Lord, Let not the wife depart from her husband:" The witnesses note the Lord's command against the wife's departing. On "I command, yet not I, but the Lord," the grammatical voices note the authority. Barnes and Robertson note Paul distinguishes this from his own counsel: here he commands not on his own authority but on the Lord's — referring to Christ's own teaching on marriage and divorce (Matthew 19:6-9; Mark 10:11-12).

On "let not the wife depart from her husband," the witnesses note the prohibition. Barnes and Henry note the command: the wife is not to depart (separate herself, leave) from her husband — the marriage bond is not to be dissolved at will.

The Reformed Calvin and puritan Henry note the weight: this rests on the Lord's express word, the permanence of marriage being his ordinance; the believer is bound by Christ's command, not mere apostolic advice. The verse displays the Lord's command against the wife's departing.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul distinguishes this from his own counsel — he commands not on his own authority but on the Lord's, referring to Christ's own teaching on marriage and divorce (Matthew 19; Mark 10, Barnes, Robertson); the command being that the wife is not to depart (separate, leave) from her husband, the bond not dissolved at will (Barnes, Henry). Calvin and Henry note this rests on the Lord's express word, the permanence of marriage his ordinance. The verse displays the Lord's command. The verse marks the authority: not I, but the Lord. The verse displays the prohibition: let not the wife depart. The verse displays the source: Christ's teaching. The verse displays the weight: a divine command. The command displays the permanence of the marriage bond by the Lord's word. The verse displays the authority: the Lord's. The verse displays the wife: not to depart. The verse displays the bond: not dissolved at will. The Lord's command against the wife's departing displays the permanence of marriage resting on Christ's own word: not on Paul's counsel but on the Lord's express teaching, the wife is forbidden to separate from her husband. The verse displays the divine authority behind the marriage bond's permanence, and provides for the case of separation (v.11).
11:
"But and if she depart, let her remain unmarried, or be reconciled to her husband: and let not the husband put away his wife." The witnesses note the rule for separation: remain unmarried or be reconciled. On "if she depart, let her remain unmarried, or be reconciled," the grammatical voices note the provision. Barnes and Robertson note that if a wife does separate (contrary to the command), she has only two lawful options: to remain unmarried, or to be reconciled to her husband — she is not free to marry another while he lives.

On "let not the husband put away his wife," the witnesses note the parallel duty. Barnes notes the same binds the husband: he is not to put away (divorce, dismiss) his wife — the obligation mutual.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the principle: even where separation occurs, the marriage bond remains; reconciliation is the goal, and remarriage is excluded while both live — the permanence of marriage upheld. The verse displays the rule for separation: remain unmarried or be reconciled.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: if a wife does separate (contrary to the command), she has only two lawful options — to remain unmarried or to be reconciled to her husband, not free to marry another while he lives (Barnes, Robertson); and the same binds the husband, who is not to put away (divorce) his wife, the obligation mutual (Barnes). Calvin and Wesley note that even where separation occurs the bond remains, reconciliation the goal, remarriage excluded while both live. The verse displays the rule for separation. The verse marks the options: remain unmarried or reconciled. The verse displays the parallel: the husband too. The verse displays the principle: the bond remains. The verse displays the goal: reconciliation. The rule displays the bond unbroken even in separation. The verse displays the wife: unmarried or reconciled. The verse displays the husband: not to divorce. The verse displays the permanence: upheld. The rule for separation — remain unmarried or be reconciled — displays the enduring marriage bond: even where a wife wrongly departs, she may only stay single or return, not remarry while her husband lives; and the husband is likewise forbidden to divorce. The verse displays the permanence of marriage even amid separation, and turns to the case of mixed marriages (vv.12-16).
12:
"But to the rest speak I, not the Lord: If any brother hath a wife that believeth not, and she be pleased to dwell with him, let him not put her away." The witnesses note Paul's counsel on mixed marriages where the unbeliever consents. On "to the rest speak I, not the Lord," the grammatical voices note the authority. Barnes and Robertson note Paul now addresses a case the Lord had not directly spoken to — mixed marriages between a believer and an unbeliever — and gives his own apostolic (yet Spirit-guided, v.40) judgment.

On "if any brother hath a wife that believeth not... let him not put her away," the witnesses note the counsel. Barnes and Calvin note that if a believing husband has an unbelieving wife content (pleased) to dwell with him, he is not to put her away — the difference of religion is no ground for divorce, so long as she is willing to remain.

The Reformed Calvin notes the distinction: this concerns maintaining a marriage already contracted, not forming a new one with an unbeliever (which 2 Cor 6:14 forbids); the existing bond is to be kept. The verse displays Paul's counsel on mixed marriages where the unbeliever consents.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul now addresses a case the Lord had not directly spoken to — mixed marriages between believer and unbeliever — giving his own apostolic (yet Spirit-guided) judgment (Barnes, Robertson); if a believing husband has an unbelieving wife content to dwell with him, he is not to put her away, difference of religion being no ground for divorce while she is willing (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes this concerns maintaining a marriage already contracted, not forming a new one with an unbeliever. The verse displays Paul's counsel on mixed marriages. The verse marks the authority: I, not the Lord. The verse displays the case: an unbelieving wife content to dwell. The verse displays the counsel: put her not away. The verse displays the distinction: maintaining, not contracting. The counsel displays the mixed marriage kept where the unbeliever consents. The verse displays the judgment: Paul's. The verse displays the willingness: of the unbeliever. The verse displays the bond: not dissolved. Paul's counsel on mixed marriages where the unbeliever consents displays his Spirit-guided judgment on a case beyond the Lord's express word: a believing husband is not to divorce an unbelieving wife content to remain, difference of religion being no ground for separation. The verse displays the keeping of an existing mixed marriage, and extends the same rule to the believing wife (v.13).
13:
"And the woman which hath an husband that believeth not, and if he be pleased to dwell with her, let her not leave him." The brief verse notes the same rule for the believing wife. The grammatical voices note the parallel. Barnes and Robertson note Paul applies the same counsel reciprocally: a believing woman with an unbelieving husband, if he is content (pleased) to dwell with her, is not to leave him — the rule the same as for the believing husband (v.12).

On the mutuality, the witnesses note the equality. Barnes notes the symmetry of the counsel: whether the believer be husband or wife, the obligation to maintain the marriage holds equally, so long as the unbeliever consents.

The Reformed Calvin notes the point: the believing party, of either sex, is not defiled by the union nor obliged to dissolve it; the marriage stands. The verse displays the same rule for the believing wife.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul applies the same counsel reciprocally — a believing woman with an unbelieving husband content to dwell with her is not to leave him, the rule the same as for the believing husband (Barnes, Robertson); the symmetry showing the obligation holds equally whether the believer be husband or wife, so long as the unbeliever consents (Barnes). Calvin notes the believing party of either sex is not defiled nor obliged to dissolve the union. The verse displays the same rule for the wife. The verse marks the parallel: the believing woman. The verse displays the condition: the husband content to dwell. The verse displays the counsel: leave him not. The verse displays the equality: the same obligation. The rule displays the reciprocal duty in mixed marriages. The verse displays the wife: not to leave. The verse displays the husband: content to dwell. The verse displays the symmetry: equal obligation. The same rule for the believing wife displays the reciprocal obligation in mixed marriages: as the believing husband is not to put away an unbelieving wife, so the believing wife is not to leave an unbelieving husband content to dwell with her. The verse displays the equal duty of either believing spouse to maintain the union, and grounds it in the sanctifying effect of the believing partner (v.14).
14:
"For the unbelieving husband is sanctified by the wife, and the unbelieving wife is sanctified by the husband: else were your children unclean; but now are they holy." The witnesses note the sanctification of the unbelieving spouse and children — the paedobaptist crux. On "the unbelieving husband is sanctified by the wife," the grammatical voices note the meaning. Barnes and Robertson note this is not that the unbeliever is saved or made personally holy by the believing spouse, but that the marriage relation itself is sanctified (made lawful, not defiling), so the believer need not separate.

On "else were your children unclean; but now are they holy," the great debate divides. The grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson read "holy"/"unclean" as legitimate/illegitimate — if the union were unholy, the children would be illegitimate; but they are legitimate, proving the marriage lawful; the verse throws no light on infant baptism. The Reformed Calvin reads it federally: the children of believers are set apart by the covenant, reckoned holy and in the Church (as Abraham's seed, Romans 11:16) — a ground for infant baptism.

The witnesses note the agreed core: the believing partner does not contract defilement from the unbeliever, and the union and its offspring are not impure. The verse displays the sanctification of the unbelieving spouse and children, with the paedobaptist crux.

The witnesses agree the believing spouse is not defiled by the unbeliever: the marriage relation is sanctified (made lawful, not defiling), so the believer need not separate (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin). But "your children... holy" divides them. Barnes and Robertson read holy/unclean as legitimate/illegitimate — the children's legitimacy proving the marriage lawful, the verse throwing no light on infant baptism. Calvin reads it federally — the children of believers set apart by the covenant, reckoned holy and in the Church (as Abraham's seed, Romans 11:16), a ground for paedobaptism; Clarke and Ellicott take "holy" as Christian consecration from being in the body of Christ. The verse displays the sanctification of spouse and children. The verse marks the sanctification: the unbeliever by the believer. The verse displays the children: holy, not unclean. The verse displays the debate: legitimacy vs federal holiness. The verse displays the core: the believer undefiled. The sanctification displays the union and its offspring not impure. The verse displays the spouse: sanctified. The verse displays the children: holy. The verse displays the crux: paedobaptism. The sanctification of the unbelieving spouse and children displays that the believer is not defiled by the union: the marriage is lawful and its offspring not impure — read by the grammatical-historical as the children's legitimacy (no light on baptism) and by Calvin as their federal, covenantal holiness grounding infant baptism. The verse displays the believer's freedom to remain, and provides for the case where the unbeliever departs (v.15).
15:
"But if the unbelieving depart, let him depart. A brother or a sister is not under bondage in such cases: but God hath called us to peace." The witnesses note the believer's freedom when the unbeliever departs — the Pauline privilege. On "if the unbelieving depart, let him depart," the grammatical voices note the case. Barnes and Robertson note that if the unbelieving spouse insists on leaving (deserting the believer, refusing to dwell together), the believer is to let them go — not to compel the union by force.

On "a brother or a sister is not under bondage," the great debate divides. The grammatical-historical Barnes reads "not under bondage" narrowly: the believer is not bound to make provision for or to compel cohabitation with the deserter — but is NOT thereby free to remarry. The Reformed Calvin (with Luther, Grotius) reads it as freedom to remarry: the bond is utterly broken by the unbeliever's desertion, so the deserted believer may marry again.

On "God hath called us to peace," the witnesses note the principle: the believer is to seek peace, not contention — accepting the separation rather than forcing an impossible union. The verse displays the believer's freedom when the unbeliever departs, with the remarriage crux.

The witnesses agree: if the unbelieving spouse insists on leaving, the believer is to let them go, not compelling the union, for "God hath called us to peace" — the believer seeking peace rather than contention (Barnes, Robertson). On "they that buy, as though they possessed not," the Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the detachment in possessions: those who buy (acquire property) are to hold it as if they possessed nothing — using earthly goods without clinging to them, since all is transitory. The verse displays holding sorrow, joy, and possessions loosely.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree Paul continues the theme of detachment: those who weep are to be as though they wept not (not overwhelmed by passing sorrow), those who rejoice as though they rejoiced not (not absorbed in fleeting earthly joy), and those who buy as though they possessed not (using goods without clinging, since all is transitory, Barnes, Robertson). Calvin and Wesley note the believer is to use earthly things without being engrossed by them. The verse displays holding all loosely. The verse marks the sorrow: as though not weeping. 31:
"And they that use this world, as not abusing it: for the fashion of this world passeth away." The witnesses note using the world without abusing it, since its fashion passes. On "they that use this world, as not abusing it," the grammatical voices note the moderation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul concludes the series: those who use this world (its goods, relations, comforts) are not to "abuse" it — that is, not to use it to the full, to over-use, misuse, or be engrossed by it; lawful use without excess or absorption.

On "the fashion of this world passeth away," the witnesses note the reason. Barnes and Calvin note the ground: the "fashion" (schema, the outward form, the passing scene, as of a stage-show or shifting scenery) of this world is passing away — transitory, soon to vanish; therefore not to be clung to.

The Reformed Calvin notes the principle: the transience of the world is the reason for holding it loosely; the believer uses present things as a traveller, not a settler, the heart fixed on what abides. The verse displays using the world without abusing it, since its fashion passes.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: those who use this world (its goods, relations, comforts) are not to "abuse" it — not to over-use, misuse, or be engrossed by it, but to use it lawfully without excess or absorption (Barnes, Robertson); for the "fashion" (schema, the outward form, the passing scene) of this world is passing away, transitory and soon to vanish (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes the believer uses present things as a traveller, not a settler, the heart fixed on what abides. The verse displays using the world without abusing it. 32:
"But I would have you without carefulness. He that is unmarried careth for the things that belong to the Lord, how he may please the Lord:" The witnesses note the unmarried man's freedom to care for the Lord's things. On "I would have you without carefulness," the grammatical voices note Paul's aim. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's desire: to have them "without carefulness" (free from anxiety, distracting cares) — the great reason behind his counsel about the single state.

On "the unmarried careth for the things of the Lord, how he may please the Lord," the witnesses note the advantage. Barnes and Calvin note the unmarried man is free to care for (be devoted to) the things of the Lord — how he may please the Lord — his attention undivided, not drawn off by family concerns.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the principle: the single state, rightly used, affords freedom for undistracted devotion to God; this, not any superior holiness, is its advantage. The verse displays the unmarried man's freedom to care for the Lord's things.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's desire is to have them "without carefulness" (free from anxiety, distracting cares) — the reason behind his counsel (Barnes, Robertson); the unmarried man being free to care for the things of the Lord, how he may please him, his attention undivided, not drawn off by family concerns (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin and Wesley note the single state, rightly used, affords freedom for undistracted devotion — this, not superior holiness, its advantage. The verse displays the unmarried man's freedom. The verse displays the principle: undivided devotion. The freedom displays the single state's advantage of undistracted devotion. The verse displays the freedom: from anxiety. The verse displays the care: for the Lord. The verse displays the pleasing: of God. The verse displays the undistracted devotion the single life allows, and contrasts it with the married man's divided care (v.33).
33:
"But he that is married careth for the things that are of the world, how he may please his wife." The brief verse notes the married man's divided care for the world and his wife. The grammatical voices note the contrast. Barnes and Robertson note the married man, by contrast, cares for "the things of the world" — the temporal affairs, the providing for and pleasing of his wife and family — his attention necessarily divided.

On "how he may please his wife," the witnesses note the lawful concern. Barnes notes this is no fault but a proper duty: the married man rightly seeks to please his wife and provide for his household — yet this very duty draws off some of the attention the single man can give wholly to the Lord.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the balance: the division is not sin but the natural burden of the married state; Paul notes it not to condemn marriage but to show why the single state is freer in a time of distress. The verse displays the married man's divided care.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the married man, by contrast, cares for "the things of the world" — the temporal affairs, the providing for and pleasing of his wife and family — his attention necessarily divided (Barnes, Robertson); this no fault but a proper duty, the married man rightly seeking to please his wife, yet this drawing off some attention the single man gives wholly to the Lord (Barnes). Calvin and Wesley note the division is not sin but the natural burden of the married state, noted not to condemn marriage but to show the single state's freedom. 34:
"There is difference also between a wife and a virgin. The unmarried woman careth for the things of the Lord, that she may be holy both in body and in spirit: but she that is married careth for the things of the world, how she may please her husband." The witnesses note the same contrast applied to women. On "difference between a wife and a virgin," the grammatical voices note the parallel. Barnes and Robertson note Paul applies the same distinction to women: there is a difference between the married wife and the unmarried virgin in respect of their cares and devotion.

On "the unmarried woman careth for the things of the Lord... holy in body and spirit," the witnesses note the advantage. Barnes and Calvin note the unmarried woman is free to care for the Lord's things, that she may be holy (consecrated, devoted) in both body and spirit — wholly given to God's service.

On "she that is married careth... how she may please her husband," the witnesses note the parallel division: the married woman, like the married man, has the divided care of pleasing her husband and tending her household. The verse displays the same contrast applied to women.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul applies the same distinction to women — a difference between the married wife and the unmarried virgin in their cares (Barnes, Robertson); the unmarried woman free to care for the Lord's things, that she may be holy (consecrated) in body and spirit, wholly given to God (Barnes, Calvin); while the married woman has the divided care of pleasing her husband and household. The verse displays the contrast applied to women. The verse marks the difference: wife and virgin. The verse displays the unmarried: holy in body and spirit. The verse displays the married: pleasing her husband. The verse displays the parallel: with the man. The contrast displays the single woman's freedom for the Lord. The verse displays the virgin: devoted to God. The verse displays the wife: divided care. The verse displays the holiness: body and spirit. The verse displays the parallel advantage of the single life for women, and states Paul's loving purpose in the counsel (v.35).
35:
"And this I speak for your own profit; not that I may cast a snare upon you, but for that which is comely, and that ye may attend upon the Lord without distraction." The witnesses note Paul's loving purpose: their profit and undistracted devotion. On "this I speak for your own profit," the grammatical voices note the motive. Barnes and Robertson note Paul disavows any wish to bind them: he speaks all this for their own advantage (profit, benefit), not to restrict them needlessly.

On "not that I may cast a snare upon you," the witnesses note the disclaimer. Barnes and Calvin note Paul does not mean to "cast a snare" (a noose, a net) upon them — that is, not to entangle their consciences or impose celibacy as a binding obligation, a trap from which they could not escape.

On "that which is comely... attend upon the Lord without distraction," the Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the aim: Paul's whole purpose is what is seemly (comely, fitting) and that they may wait on the Lord without distraction — undivided devotion being the goal, freely chosen, not compelled. The verse displays Paul's loving purpose.

The witnesses agree: Paul disavows any wish to bind them — he speaks for their own profit, not to restrict them needlessly (Barnes, Robertson); not to "cast a snare" (a noose) on them, not entangling their consciences or imposing celibacy as a binding obligation (Barnes, Calvin); his whole aim being what is comely and that they may attend on the Lord without distraction — undivided devotion freely chosen, not compelled (Calvin, Wesley). 36:
"But if any man think that he behaveth himself uncomely toward his virgin, if she pass the flower of her age, and need so require, let him do what he will, he sinneth not: let them marry." The witnesses note the counsel about a man and his virgin — and the father/betrothed debate. On "his virgin," the great interpretive question divides. The older majority (Gill, Henry, Calvin) take it of a father (or guardian) and his unmarried daughter: if he thinks he behaves unbecomingly toward her (by keeping her unmarried past the flower of her age, to her discredit), and need requires, let him give her in marriage — he sins not.

On the alternative, the witnesses note the modern reading. Some (and increasingly Robertson, Ellicott) take it of a man and his own betrothed virgin (or one to whom he is engaged or spiritually bound): if he thinks he is treating her uncomely by withholding marriage, and passion requires, let them marry.

The witnesses agree on the substance whichever view is taken: to give in marriage (or to marry) in such circumstances is no sin. The Reformed Calvin notes the liberty: where remaining single causes hardship or dishonour, marriage is freely permitted. The verse displays the counsel about a man and his virgin, with the father/betrothed debate.

The witnesses agree the substance: to give in marriage (or to marry) in such circumstances — where keeping single causes hardship, dishonour, or unbecoming strain, and need requires — is no sin (Barnes, Calvin). But "his virgin" divides them: the older majority (Gill, Henry, Calvin) take it of a father and his unmarried daughter (giving her in marriage past the flower of her age); the modern reading (Robertson, Ellicott) takes it of a man and his own betrothed virgin (letting them marry). The verse displays the counsel about a man and his virgin. The verse marks the question: father or betrothed. The verse displays the circumstance: past the flower of her age. The verse displays the permission: let them marry, no sin. The verse displays the liberty: marriage freely allowed. The counsel displays marriage permitted where the single state strains. The verse displays the freedom to marry where circumstances require, and commends the steadfast resolve to keep the virgin single (v.37).
37:
"Nevertheless he that standeth stedfast in his heart, having no necessity, but hath power over his own will, and hath so decreed in his heart that he will keep his virgin, doeth well." The witnesses note the commendation of the steadfast resolve to keep the virgin single. On "he that standeth stedfast... having no necessity," the grammatical voices note the conditions. Barnes and Robertson note Paul commends the man who stands firm in his heart, under no necessity (no compelling circumstance, such as the virgin's strong desire to marry or danger of sin) — free to follow his settled judgment.

On "hath power over his own will... decreed to keep his virgin," the witnesses note the deliberate resolve. Barnes and Calvin note such a one has control over his own will (is master of his choice) and has firmly decided in his heart to keep his virgin (daughter or betrothed) unmarried — and in so doing "doeth well."

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the balance: the resolve to keep the virgin single is commended where it is free, deliberate, and without necessity — good, as marrying is also good (v.38); the choice is left to a settled, unconstrained judgment. The verse displays the commendation of the steadfast resolve to keep the virgin single.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul commends the man who stands firm in his heart, under no necessity (no compelling circumstance) and master of his own will, who has firmly decided to keep his virgin unmarried — and in so doing "doeth well" (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin). Calvin and Wesley note the resolve is commended where free, deliberate, and without necessity — good, as marrying is also good; the choice left to a settled, unconstrained judgment. The verse displays the commendation of the steadfast resolve. The verse marks the conditions: stedfast, no necessity. The verse displays the resolve: decreed to keep her single. The verse displays the commendation: doeth well. The verse displays the balance: free and deliberate. The commendation displays the free resolve for the single state honoured. The verse displays the steadfastness: of heart. The verse displays the freedom: from necessity. The verse displays the decree: to keep single. The commendation of the steadfast resolve to keep the virgin single displays Paul's honouring of a free, deliberate choice: the man who, under no necessity and master of his will, firmly decides to keep his virgin unmarried does well — the single state freely chosen being commended, as marrying is also good. The verse displays the worthiness of a settled resolve for celibacy, and weighs the two courses (v.38).
38:
"So then he that giveth her in marriage doeth well; but he that giveth her not in marriage doeth better." The witnesses note the comparison: giving in marriage does well, withholding does better. On "he that giveth her in marriage doeth well," the grammatical voices note the first. Barnes and Robertson note Paul declares the one who gives his virgin in marriage (or marries) does well — the course is good, lawful, and right.

On "he that giveth her not in marriage doeth better," the witnesses note the comparative. Barnes and Calvin note the one who keeps the virgin single "doeth better" — better, that is, in the circumstances of the present distress, for the freedom from care and undistracted devotion already described, not in any intrinsic holiness.

The Reformed Calvin and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the crucial qualification: the "better" is one of expediency for the time, not of moral or spiritual superiority; against the notion that celibacy is a holier state, Paul grounds the preference wholly in present circumstances. The verse displays the comparison: giving in marriage does well, withholding does better.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the one who gives his virgin in marriage (or marries) does well — the course good, lawful, and right (Barnes, Robertson); while the one who keeps the virgin single "doeth better" — better in the circumstances of the present distress, for freedom from care and undistracted devotion, not in any intrinsic holiness (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin and Wesley note the "better" is expediency for the time, not moral superiority; against the notion that celibacy is holier, the preference is grounded wholly in present circumstances. The verse displays the comparison. The verse marks the first: giving in marriage does well. The verse displays the second: withholding does better. The verse displays the ground: expediency, not holiness. The verse displays the qualification: circumstantial. The comparison displays celibacy preferred for circumstance, not merit. The verse displays the marrying: good. The verse displays the keeping single: better. The verse displays the reason: the present distress. The comparison — giving in marriage does well, withholding does better — displays Paul's measured preference: both courses are good, but the single state is "better" for the present distress, for its freedom from care, not because celibacy is holier. The verse displays the circumstantial, not intrinsic, advantage of the single life, and closes with the law of remarriage for widows (vv.39-40).
39:
"The wife is bound by the law as long as her husband liveth; but if her husband be dead, she is at liberty to be married to whom she will; only in the Lord." The witnesses note the wife bound for life, free to remarry only in the Lord. On "the wife is bound by the law as long as her husband liveth," the grammatical voices note the permanence. Barnes and Robertson note the marriage bond holds for life: the wife is bound (by the law of marriage) to her husband as long as he lives — the tie dissolved only by death.

On "if her husband be dead, she is at liberty to be married to whom she will," the witnesses note the freedom. Barnes and Henry note that on the husband's death the widow is free to remarry whom she will — remarriage of widows being lawful and permitted.

On "only in the Lord," the Reformed Calvin and puritan Henry note the one restriction: she is to marry only "in the Lord" — that is, a believer, a Christian, and in a manner consistent with her faith; the liberty is real but bounded by godliness. The verse displays the wife bound for life, free to remarry only in the Lord.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the marriage bond holds for life — the wife bound to her husband as long as he lives, the tie dissolved only by death (Barnes, Robertson); on his death the widow is free to remarry whom she will, remarriage being lawful (Barnes, Henry); but "only in the Lord" — to a believer, in a manner consistent with her faith (Calvin, Henry). The verse displays the wife bound for life. The verse marks the permanence: bound while he lives. The verse displays the freedom: free if he dies. The verse displays the restriction: only in the Lord. The verse displays the bound: godly remarriage. The bond displays marriage for life and remarriage bounded by faith. The wife bound for life, free to remarry only in the Lord, displays both the permanence of marriage and the bounded liberty of the widow: the bond holds till death, after which she may remarry, but only a believer and in a manner befitting her faith. 40:
"But she is happier if she so abide, after my judgment: and I think also that I have the Spirit of God." The witnesses note Paul's judgment that the widow is happier remaining single, with the Spirit's authority. On "she is happier if she so abide, after my judgment," the grammatical voices note the counsel. Barnes and Robertson note Paul gives his judgment (opinion) that the widow is "happier" (more blessed, more free from care) if she remains as she is — unmarried — for the reasons of freedom and undistracted devotion already given.

On "I think also that I have the Spirit of God," the witnesses note the authority. Barnes and Calvin note Paul closes by affirming that he too has the Spirit of God — his "judgment," though offered modestly ("I think"), is no mere private opinion but the counsel of an apostle guided by the Holy Spirit, and so to be received with confidence.

The Reformed Calvin notes the gentle assurance: Paul, with apostolic modesty, claims the Spirit's guidance for his counsel, that the Corinthians might heed it as Spirit-taught, not lightly dismiss it. The verse displays Paul's judgment that the widow is happier single, with the Spirit's authority.

The witnesses agree: Paul gives his judgment that the widow is "happier" (more blessed, freer from care) if she remains unmarried, for the freedom and undistracted devotion already described (Barnes, Robertson); and closes by affirming he too has the Spirit of God — his judgment, though modestly offered ("I think"), being no mere private opinion but the counsel of an apostle guided by the Spirit (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes Paul, with apostolic modesty, claims the Spirit's guidance, that the Corinthians might heed it as Spirit-taught. The verse displays Paul's judgment with the Spirit's authority. The verse marks the counsel: happier if she abide. The verse displays the authority: I have the Spirit of God. The verse displays the modesty: I think. The verse displays the weight: Spirit-guided. The verse displays the close of the chapter on marriage and the single life, sealing the apostle's counsel with the Spirit's authority.
